questionnaire.title: Starting Preschool Life Map
questionnaire.purpose: Collect only the details that meaningfully personalize a preschool preparation story: routine, relationships, worries, language, visuals, emotional arc, and coping strategies.
questionnaire.tone: Warm, expert-guided, conversational, and concise. The parent should feel supported by a child development and educational therapy lens, not screened by a generic form.
designPrinciples: Use a multi-step flow with 1 to 3 questions per screen.
Ask required questions early only when they shape the story globally.
Use branching to avoid asking about concerns that do not apply.
Prefer concrete story-making details over broad clinical history.
Avoid false reassurance. Only include promises that are true and safe.
Normalize mixed feelings while emphasizing routine, trusted adults, coping tools, and return.

questions.question: What name should we use for your child in the story?
questions.helperText: This anchors the story in your child's real identity and helps the narration feel familiar.
questions.storyImpact: Used as the main character name throughout the story.
questions.illustrationImpact: Used for any name labels, cubby tags, backpack labels, or title page personalization.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Creates immediate self-recognition and ownership of the preschool story.

questions.question: How old is your child?
questions.helperText: Age helps us choose developmentally appropriate language, pacing, and coping strategies.
questions.storyImpact: Adjusts sentence length, independence expectations, and emotional language.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides approximate body proportions and preschool-age visual details.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Keeps the story aligned with the child's developmental stage.

questions.question: What pronouns should the story use for your child?
questions.helperText: We want the story to sound natural and respectful to your family.
questions.storyImpact: Controls narration and adult dialogue.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact unless paired with photo references.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports identity safety and accurate family language.

questions.question: What pronouns or wording should we use?
questions.helperText: Share the exact wording you prefer.
questions.storyImpact: Used in all narration and dialogue.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Ensures respectful, consistent language.

questions.question: When does preschool start?
questions.helperText: The story can gently build toward a real date or stay more general if the date is not set.
questions.storyImpact: May add a countdown or 'soon' language.
questions.illustrationImpact: Can influence calendar visuals.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reduces uncertainty by giving the transition a clear time frame.

questions.question: Has your child been in daycare, preschool, or another group care setting before?
questions.helperText: This helps us know whether preschool is completely new or a familiar kind of experience.
questions.branchingLogic: If first_group_care, emphasize novelty and extra routine preview. If previous_preschool, ask prior_experience_notes.
questions.storyImpact: Adjusts whether the story frames preschool as a first-time experience or a new version of something familiar.
questions.illustrationImpact: May include comparison imagery such as home-to-school or old-classroom-to-new-classroom.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Allows the story to meet the child at the right level of novelty.

questions.question: Is there anything from that prior experience we should know?
questions.helperText: Briefly share what went well or what felt hard so we can build on it carefully.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if prior_group_care = previous_preschool or some_daycare_or_classes.
questions.storyImpact: Can reference strengths or avoid repeating difficult framing.
questions.illustrationImpact: Usually none unless a specific familiar item or setting is mentioned.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Uses past experience to support continuity and repair.

questions.question: Which description sounds most like your child in new situations?
questions.helperText: Temperament helps us choose the right emotional arc: slow warm-up, eager explorer, observer first, or flexible joiner.
questions.storyImpact: Sets the starting emotional state and pacing of confidence-building.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides facial expressions and body language across early pages.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Validates the child's natural approach instead of forcing bravery too quickly.

questions.question: How does your child usually approach other children?
questions.helperText: This helps us write friendship moments that feel believable.
questions.storyImpact: Shapes peer interaction scenes and joining-play language.
questions.illustrationImpact: Determines whether the child is shown nearby, joining, watching, or playing with one peer.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports social confidence without implying one correct way to make friends.

questions.question: How sensitive is your child to noise, crowds, clothing, smells, or busy spaces?
questions.helperText: Preschool can be sensory-rich. This helps us include supports if needed.
questions.branchingLogic: If somewhat_sensitive, very_sensitive, or sensory_seeking, show sensory_sensitivity and sensory_needs.
questions.storyImpact: Adds sensory-aware descriptions and coping choices.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show quieter corners, headphones, fidgets, or calm spaces.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Prepares for sensory load and gives the child language for regulation.

questions.question: What does your child love right now?
questions.helperText: Favorite interests make the story feel like it was written for your child, not about any child.
questions.storyImpact: Adds motivating details, metaphors, play scenes, and page-specific personalization.
questions.illustrationImpact: May include favorite colors, toys, topics, animals, characters, vehicles, or pretend play themes.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Uses familiar joy as an emotional bridge into an unfamiliar setting.

questions.question: What usually makes your child laugh?
questions.helperText: A small moment of humor can help the story feel warm and emotionally safe.
questions.storyImpact: Adds a playful beat during a neutral or mildly stressful scene.
questions.illustrationImpact: May inspire a silly expression, playful object, or funny classroom moment.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Pairs the preschool setting with positive affect and connection.

questions.question: What feels hardest or most uncertain about preschool right now?
questions.helperText: Choose anything that applies. We will only ask follow-up questions for the areas you select.
questions.branchingLogic: Controls all concern-specific follow-up screens. If no_worries_expressed is selected, hide the other follow-up branches unless the parent also selects a concern.
questions.storyImpact: Defines the central emotional challenge and resolution arc.
questions.illustrationImpact: Determines which preschool scenes need emphasis.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Targets the story toward the child's most meaningful uncertainty.

questions.question: Is there anything you are quietly wondering about, even if your child has not mentioned it?
questions.helperText: Sometimes the story can gently preview a routine before it becomes a worry.
questions.storyImpact: May add subtle preparation without labeling the child as worried.
questions.illustrationImpact: May influence which routine scenes appear.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Allows proactive preparation without projecting anxiety onto the child.

questions.question: Has your child met or seen the teacher yet?
questions.helperText: This helps us decide whether the teacher should be introduced as familiar or new.
questions.storyImpact: Shapes teacher introduction language.
questions.illustrationImpact: May include teacher photo reference if available.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds predictability around a new trusted adult.

questions.question: What would you like the story to show the teacher helping with?
questions.helperText: Children often feel safer when they know exactly how a teacher can help.
questions.storyImpact: Adds teacher support scenes and dialogue.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows the teacher near the child in concrete helpful moments.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Transfers some safety from caregiver to teacher.

questions.question: What part of making friends might feel hardest?
questions.helperText: Friendship support is most useful when it matches the actual social challenge.
questions.storyImpact: Creates a realistic peer moment and success step.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show parallel play, one friend, group play, or adult-supported entry.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Gives the child a concrete, non-pressured way to connect.

questions.question: What words could your child practice for joining play?
questions.helperText: A simple script can make social entry feel less mysterious.
questions.storyImpact: Adds child-friendly dialogue such as 'Can I play?' or 'What are you building?'
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows the child using words or gestures to join.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds social problem-solving and reduces uncertainty.

questions.question: What kind of bathroom help might your child need at school?
questions.helperText: We can keep this matter-of-fact and reassuring.
questions.storyImpact: Adds practical bathroom routine and help-request language.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show bathroom doorway, handwashing, or teacher nearby without exposing private details.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reduces shame and uncertainty around toileting.

questions.question: What do you know about the school bathroom routine?
questions.helperText: Even small details can help the story preview what will happen.
questions.storyImpact: Adds accurate sequence around asking, going, flushing, washing, and returning.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show sink, handwashing, or bathroom location.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Turns an uncertain private task into a predictable routine.

questions.question: Which sensory experiences are most likely to bother or overwhelm your child?
questions.helperText: This helps us include the right supports without making the classroom sound scary.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if main_concerns includes loud_classroom or sensitivity_level indicates sensory concern.
questions.storyImpact: Adds sensory-aware narration and coping options.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show quieter area, headphones, teacher support, or distance from noise.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Validates sensory needs and gives the child a plan.

questions.question: What helps when things get too loud or busy?
questions.helperText: We can build the child's actual regulation tools into the preschool scene.
questions.storyImpact: Adds coping sequence during a busy classroom moment.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows specific support tools or calm space.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports self-advocacy and nervous-system regulation.

questions.question: Which parts of the preschool day would be most helpful to preview?
questions.helperText: Some children feel calmer when they can picture the order of the day.
questions.storyImpact: Determines which routine beats receive extra page space.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides key scene selection.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Creates predictability and reduces cognitive load.

questions.question: What helps your child move from one activity to another?
questions.helperText: Transitions are often where preschool feels hardest.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if main_concerns includes not_knowing_routine or following_directions.
questions.storyImpact: Adds transition language and classroom rhythm.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show visual schedule, timer, or teacher cue.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports flexibility and preparedness.

questions.question: What should we know about snack or lunch?
questions.helperText: Food routines can involve independence, sensory preferences, allergies, or comfort from home.
questions.storyImpact: Adds accurate lunch/snack expectations and reassurance.
questions.illustrationImpact: May include lunchbox, water bottle, or preferred foods.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reduces uncertainty around eating away from home.

questions.question: What help might your child need with food or drinks?
questions.helperText: Small practical supports can prevent a worry from becoming the center of the day.
questions.storyImpact: Adds help-seeking and independence moments.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows lunchbox, containers, table, or teacher support.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds confidence around self-care routines.

questions.question: How does your child usually handle nap or rest time?
questions.helperText: Rest time can be easy, unfamiliar, or emotionally loaded.
questions.storyImpact: Sets expectations for rest without requiring sleep.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show mat, blanket, quiet book, dim room, or calm teacher.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Separates resting from pressure to sleep and supports body calm.

questions.question: What helps your child feel calm during rest?
questions.helperText: We can include real comforts if the school allows them.
questions.storyImpact: Adds rest-time coping tools and reassurance.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows comfort object or rest setup.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports co-regulation and quiet-body practice.

questions.question: What helps your child follow directions?
questions.helperText: This lets the story show support rather than scolding.
questions.storyImpact: Adds teacher prompts and child success moments.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show visual cues, teacher modeling, or child following a simple step.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Frames compliance as skill-building and support.

questions.question: What words should the story give your child for asking for help?
questions.helperText: A simple phrase can make adult support feel reachable.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if main_concerns includes following_directions, new_teacher, bathroom, or eating_lunch_snack.
questions.storyImpact: Adds repeated help-seeking phrase.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show child speaking to teacher.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds self-advocacy and reduces helplessness.

questions.question: Who will usually drop your child off at preschool?
questions.helperText: Drop-off is often the most important emotional moment in the story.
questions.storyImpact: Identifies the adult in the goodbye scene.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides which caregiver appears at arrival.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Makes the separation scene concrete and emotionally truthful.

questions.question: What will drop-off probably look like?
questions.helperText: Even if the exact routine is not set, share what you know.
questions.storyImpact: Creates the arrival and goodbye sequence.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows car line, classroom door, hallway, cubby, or playground arrival.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reduces uncertainty by previewing the sequence.

questions.question: What will you say before leaving?
questions.helperText: A consistent goodbye phrase can become a bridge between home and school.
questions.storyImpact: Used as repeated caregiver dialogue.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show hug, wave, high five, or goodbye ritual.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Creates a predictable separation cue.

questions.question: What does your child usually do when separating from you?
questions.helperText: We can write a scene that validates the real feeling and moves toward support.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if main_concerns includes separation_from_parent or fear_of_being_left.
questions.storyImpact: Shapes the emotional peak and adult response.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides facial expression and body posture in goodbye scene.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Normalizes separation responses and models co-regulation.

questions.question: What reassurance is true and safe to include?
questions.helperText: We only want to include promises that will reliably happen.
questions.branchingLogic: Always shown; extra important if separation concern is selected.
questions.storyImpact: Provides core reassurance language.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show reunion or teacher support.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds trust through truthful, repeatable safety messages.

questions.question: Who will pick your child up or come back at the end of the school day?
questions.helperText: Children often need the return to be named clearly.
questions.storyImpact: Sets the reunion ending.
questions.illustrationImpact: Determines pickup adult illustration.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reinforces object permanence and the return of trusted adults.

questions.question: Is there anything we should not promise or say about drop-off?
questions.helperText: For example: 'I will stay outside,' 'You will not cry,' or 'I will come right back' unless those are true.
questions.storyImpact: Prevents inaccurate reassurance.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Protects trust and avoids reassurance that could backfire.

questions.question: What is the preschool or program called?
questions.helperText: A real school name helps the story feel specific and familiar.
questions.storyImpact: Used in title, narration, or arrival page.
questions.illustrationImpact: May appear on school sign or classroom label.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds familiarity with the place.

questions.question: What is the teacher's name, if you know it?
questions.helperText: Naming the teacher can make them feel less like a stranger.
questions.storyImpact: Used in teacher dialogue and reassurance.
questions.illustrationImpact: Can pair with teacher photo if uploaded and allowed.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Introduces a trusted school adult.

questions.question: Does the classroom have a name?
questions.helperText: Some children love knowing they are in the Bears Room, Sunflower Room, or another named class.
questions.storyImpact: Adds classroom-specific wording.
questions.illustrationImpact: May appear on a classroom sign.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Makes the new environment easier to picture.

questions.question: What happens first when your child arrives?
questions.helperText: The first few minutes shape the child's sense of safety.
questions.storyImpact: Builds the opening school sequence.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows classroom entrance, sign-in, cubby, or greeting.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Previews the beginning of the school day.

questions.question: Where will your child put their backpack or belongings?
questions.helperText: Belongings routines give children a concrete job when they arrive.
questions.storyImpact: Adds a small competence moment.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show cubby, hook, bin, or backpack.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports independence and arrival predictability.

questions.question: Which parts of the preschool day should the story include?
questions.helperText: Choose what actually happens or is likely to happen.
questions.branchingLogic: Show detail questions for selected routine parts if not already covered by concern branches.
questions.storyImpact: Defines the middle pages of the story.
questions.illustrationImpact: Determines major scene list.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Gives the child a mental map of the day.

questions.question: What will pickup look like?
questions.helperText: The story should end with the real return as clearly as possible.
questions.storyImpact: Creates the reunion ending.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows pickup location and adult.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reinforces that the child is not left and the day has an ending.

questions.question: What helps your child calm when feelings get big?
questions.helperText: We can include strategies your child already knows, not introduce too many new ones.
questions.storyImpact: Selects the coping strategy used at the emotional turning point.
questions.illustrationImpact: Shows the child using a regulation tool.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds concrete self-regulation and co-regulation scripts.

questions.question: Does your child have a comfort object?
questions.helperText: A familiar object can be a powerful bridge if it is realistic to include.
questions.storyImpact: May include the object in home, backpack, drop-off, or rest scenes.
questions.illustrationImpact: Can be illustrated from photo reference.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports transitional object use and emotional continuity.

questions.question: Can this comfort object go to school?
questions.helperText: We do not want the story to promise access to something the school will not allow.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if comfort_object has an answer.
questions.storyImpact: Determines where and how the object appears.
questions.illustrationImpact: Places object in hand, backpack, cubby, rest mat, or home-only goodbye ritual.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Keeps comfort plans truthful and usable.

questions.question: Is there a breathing tool or calming practice your child already knows?
questions.helperText: Examples: smell the flower/blow the candle, balloon belly, hand breathing.
questions.storyImpact: May include this exact tool during a nervous moment.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show gesture or body posture.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reinforces a familiar regulation strategy.

questions.question: Is there a song, phrase, or small ritual that comforts your child?
questions.helperText: Family language can make the story feel emotionally connected.
questions.storyImpact: Can become a repeated refrain or parent note.
questions.illustrationImpact: May show gesture or ritual such as kiss on hand.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Creates continuity between caregiver comfort and school coping.

questions.question: Are there sensory needs or supports we should include?
questions.helperText: Share only what would help the story show your child's body feeling safe.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if sensitivity_level indicates sensory concern or main_concerns includes loud_classroom.
questions.storyImpact: Adds sensory supports and self-advocacy language.
questions.illustrationImpact: May include headphones, chew, fidget, quiet corner, weighted item, or space from crowd.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Affirms sensory needs and builds a realistic support plan.

questions.question: Which caregivers should be named in the story?
questions.helperText: Use the names or titles your child actually uses.
questions.storyImpact: Controls family references and dialogue.
questions.illustrationImpact: Determines possible caregiver characters.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Connects the child's secure base to the preschool transition.

questions.question: Are there siblings, pets, grandparents, or other important people your child may want included?
questions.helperText: Include people or pets only if they meaningfully support the story.
questions.storyImpact: May add home scenes, encouragement, or pickup references.
questions.illustrationImpact: Can include family members, pets, or important adults if photos are provided.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Adds attachment figures and familiar emotional anchors.

questions.question: Who should appear in the story?
questions.helperText: This helps us keep the cast focused and comforting.
questions.storyImpact: Sets the active character list.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides illustration references and scene composition.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Prioritizes trusted figures who support emotional safety.

questions.question: Is there anyone who should not appear or be mentioned?
questions.helperText: This protects your child's emotional safety and your family's privacy.
questions.storyImpact: Prevents unwanted references.
questions.illustrationImpact: Prevents unwanted visual inclusion.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Avoids sensitive or dysregulating content.

questions.question: What does your child call their caregivers?
questions.helperText: The exact words matter: Mama, Mommy, Dad, Papa, Nana, Gigi, and so on.
questions.storyImpact: Used in dialogue and narration.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Makes reassurance sound like home.

questions.question: What words does your family use for the bathroom or potty?
questions.helperText: Using familiar words helps this part feel matter-of-fact.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if main_concerns includes using_bathroom or routine_known_parts includes bathroom.
questions.storyImpact: Controls bathroom wording.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Reduces embarrassment and improves comprehension.

questions.question: What should the story call school adults?
questions.helperText: For example: teacher, grown-up, Miss Ana, Mr. Lee, helper.
questions.storyImpact: Controls adult labels in school scenes.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds clear categories for trusted adults.

questions.question: What should we call preschool in the story?
questions.helperText: Some families say school, preschool, class, little school, or the school name.
questions.storyImpact: Sets consistent school wording.
questions.illustrationImpact: May influence labels.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Keeps the story aligned with the words the child hears at home.

questions.question: Are there any family phrases we should use?
questions.helperText: Short, familiar phrases can become powerful story anchors.
questions.storyImpact: May become refrain, goodbye phrase, or coping phrase.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Uses familiar language to create emotional safety.

questions.question: Are there any words or phrases we should avoid?
questions.helperText: Examples: big kid, brave, don't cry, scary, shy, baby, or anything that does not fit your child.
questions.storyImpact: Prevents unwanted framing.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Avoids shame, pressure, or triggering language.

questions.question: Upload a clear photo of your child for face and body reference.
questions.helperText: Required. This helps the illustration feel recognizably like your child while preserving a storybook style.
questions.storyImpact: No text impact unless visual details are described.
questions.illustrationImpact: Primary reference for child likeness, hair, skin tone, body shape, glasses, mobility aids, and style.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Self-recognition helps the child see themself succeeding in the transition.

questions.question: Upload photos of the caregiver who drops off and the caregiver who picks up, if they should appear.
questions.helperText: Highly recommended. These photos support the goodbye and reunion scenes.
questions.storyImpact: May reinforce correct caregiver roles.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides caregiver likeness in drop-off and pickup scenes.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Makes separation and reunion visually concrete.

questions.question: Upload any school items your child will recognize.
questions.helperText: Highly recommended: backpack, lunchbox, water bottle, comfort object, cubby, or playground.
questions.storyImpact: May mention familiar belongings.
questions.illustrationImpact: Adds recognizable objects that make preschool feel familiar.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Uses concrete familiar cues to reduce novelty.

questions.question: Upload photos of the school or classroom if you have them.
questions.helperText: Highly recommended: school exterior, classroom, teacher if allowed, cubby area, playground.
questions.storyImpact: May add place-specific details.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides accurate visual preview of school spaces.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Builds environmental familiarity before the first day.

questions.question: Upload a family photo or pet photo if they will be included.
questions.helperText: Optional. Include these only if they support the story.
questions.branchingLogic: Shown if siblings_pets_adults or should_appear mentions family members or pets.
questions.storyImpact: May support home scene references.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides likeness for family or pet appearances.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Adds familiar attachment anchors when helpful.

questions.question: What would success look like after reading this story together?
questions.helperText: Choose the outcomes that matter most to your family.
questions.storyImpact: Prioritizes ending message, repeated phrases, and resolution.
questions.illustrationImpact: Highlights success scenes aligned with goals.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Connects personalization choices to functional emotional outcomes.

questions.question: If the story could help with one thing most, what would it be?
questions.helperText: This helps us focus the emotional arc instead of trying to do everything at once.
questions.storyImpact: Sets the primary therapeutic throughline.
questions.illustrationImpact: Guides which scene receives the most emotional emphasis.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Maintains a clear intervention target.

questions.question: How long should the story feel?
questions.helperText: Shorter stories are easier to reread often. Longer stories allow deeper routine preview.
questions.storyImpact: Controls number of scenes and level of detail.
questions.illustrationImpact: Controls illustration count and pacing.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Matches attention span and repetition needs.

questions.question: What tone would fit your child best?
questions.helperText: Tone changes how gently or playfully the story approaches the transition.
questions.storyImpact: Adjusts voice, pacing, humor, and emotional intensity.
questions.illustrationImpact: Influences expression, color energy, and scene mood.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Matches the child's regulation needs and family style.

questions.question: Which illustration style do you prefer?
questions.helperText: Choose the look that your child is most likely to enjoy and recognize.
questions.storyImpact: No direct text impact.
questions.illustrationImpact: Sets visual style direction.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports engagement and repeated reading.

questions.question: Would you like a parent note included at the end?
questions.helperText: A parent note can give you language for rereading and practicing the routine.
questions.branchingLogic: If yes, generate parent note using main concern and coping strategy.
questions.storyImpact: Adds or omits adult-facing guidance.
questions.illustrationImpact: No direct visual impact.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Supports parent-led repetition and co-regulation.

questions.question: How do you plan to use the story?
questions.helperText: This helps us format the book for real-life use.
questions.storyImpact: May include teacher-share note if selected.
questions.illustrationImpact: May affect page layout and print-safe details.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Encourages repeated access in the contexts where the child needs support.

questions.question: Is there anything we should avoid including?
questions.helperText: Share anything that may upset, confuse, or feel unhelpful for your child.
questions.storyImpact: Removes or reframes sensitive content.
questions.illustrationImpact: Prevents unwanted visuals.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Protects emotional safety.

questions.question: Is there anything sensitive happening in your child's life that we should handle carefully?
questions.helperText: Only share what feels relevant to the preschool story.
questions.storyImpact: Guides gentle wording and prevents unintended triggers.
questions.illustrationImpact: May prevent or adjust family scenes.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Maintains attachment safety and avoids accidental harm.

questions.question: Is there anything your child is not ready to hear or practice yet?
questions.helperText: We can keep the story developmentally honest without pushing too far.
questions.storyImpact: Limits advanced concepts or unwanted goals.
questions.illustrationImpact: May remove certain scenes.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Prevents overexposure and respects readiness.

questions.question: Are there developmental, language, sensory, disability, or neurodivergent needs we should know about?
questions.helperText: This helps us adjust language, pacing, visuals, and supports respectfully.
questions.storyImpact: Adjusts vocabulary, processing time, communication style, and support scripts.
questions.illustrationImpact: May include AAC, glasses, hearing devices, mobility supports, headphones, or other accurate details.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Makes the story accessible, affirming, and useful for the child's actual needs.

questions.question: Is there anything else you want us to know before we create the story?
questions.helperText: Use this space for details that did not fit anywhere else.
questions.storyImpact: May refine personalization or flag review needs.
questions.illustrationImpact: May refine visual references.
questions.therapeuticPurpose: Gives the parent a final sense of agency and care.

recommendedScreenOrder.note: Briefly explain that the form asks only what will shape the story.
Show only questions triggered by selected concerns, max 3 at a time.

minimumViableQuestionnaire.description: The shortest version that can still produce a meaningful preschool preparation story.

premiumDeeperPersonalization.description: The complete version for stronger visual accuracy, richer emotional fit, and more precise therapeutic support.
emphasis: Family language and words to avoid
Photos of school, belongings, teacher if allowed, and pickup/drop-off adults
Developmental, language, disability, and neurodivergent needs

answers.prior_experience_notes: She did a weekly music class and usually watched for a few minutes before joining.
answers.favorite_interests: Dinosaurs, purple shoes, bubbles, and pretending to be a doctor.
answers.makes_child_laugh: Silly rhymes and when Daddy uses a funny dinosaur voice.
answers.joining_play_support: Can I play too? What are you making?
answers.dropoff_routine: Mama will walk Maya to the classroom door, help her hang up her backpack, give one hug, and say goodbye.
answers.goodbye_words: I love you. I always come back after school.
answers.what_not_to_promise: Do not say Mama will stay nearby or that Maya will not cry.
answers.arrival_routine: Hang backpack on a hook, put water bottle in the basket, then choose a table activity.
answers.cubby_backpack_location: A hook with Maya's name and a sunflower picture.
answers.pickup_routine: Mama waits outside the classroom door. Ms. Ana calls Maya's name, and Maya brings her backpack.
answers.breathing_tool: Smell the flower, blow the candle.
answers.comfort_song_or_phrase: Mama says, 'You can miss me and still have a good day.'
answers.siblings_pets_adults: Baby brother Leo and Grandma Ruth. Dog named Pickle.
answers.should_appear: Mama, Daddy, Ms. Ana, baby Leo in the home scene, and Pickle if there is room.
answers.should_not_appear: No extended family besides Grandma Ruth.
answers.family_phrases: You can do hard things with help.
answers.words_to_avoid: Do not call Maya shy or tell her to be a big girl.
answers.most_important_goal: Maya understands that Mama leaves and comes back after preschool.
answers.avoid_content: Avoid crowded illustrations that look chaotic.
answers.sensitive_context: Maya has been clingier since baby Leo was born.
answers.not_ready_to_hear: Do not imply she has to stay all day happily.
answers.developmental_needs: Maya needs extra processing time and simple one-step language.

outline.purpose: Start from familiarity before introducing preschool.
Name the transition and place it in time.
Give the child a concrete first job.
Validate separation feelings and use truthful reassurance.
Transfer support from caregiver to teacher.
Address the selected emotional or practical challenge directly.
Show the child using a realistic support.
Show confidence growing without requiring perfect happiness.
Reinforce that trusted adults come back.
Give the family a line to practice before school.
Help the caregiver reread the story therapeutically.

safetyRules: Never promise that the child will not cry, will love school immediately, or that a caregiver will stay nearby unless the parent explicitly confirms it is true.
Do not frame crying, clinging, sensory overwhelm, accidents, or needing help as failure.
Use 'brave' carefully and avoid it if words_to_avoid includes brave or pressure-based language.
If family structure or sensitive context is provided, keep references precise and avoid assumptions.
If developmental_needs mentions AAC, language delay, neurodivergence, disability, or sensory supports, reflect needs affirmingly and concretely.
